title: Lesson 342
transcript: Continuing our discussion of physical security, a really important question you're gonna have to answer is, how do you decide which physical security controls to put in place? In the next few videos here, we're gonna be talking through all sorts of different physical security controls. Camera systems, locks, doors, windows, generators, UPSs, fire suppression systems, all sorts of stuff. How do you decide which of those controls to put in place? Which ones are cost justified? You of course do not have an unlimited budget, so you're gonna have to be selective. Fundamentally, what you're gonna need is a process to help you figure this out. And in fact, we've already talked about a really good process that you could use to do exactly what you need to do here, which is identify what are your most valuable assets, figure out what the risks are associated with those assets, and then figure out what controls, what mitigating controls would, well, how to treat those risks You know, maybe either avoid, transfer, mitigate, or accept. And specifically, you know, mitigation. What mitigating controls are you going to put in place to mitigate the risks? And of course, that process is risk management, which we've already talked to. You've already seen this exact slide, right? And what's fundamentally, what are we doing in risk management? We're identifying our most valuable assets. We're identifying the risks associated with those assets. And then we're treating those risks. That's the process that you want to use here. But in physical security, we don't call this risk management. What we call it is the security survey. Fundamentally, it's the same process as risk management. But to be cool and different in physical security, we give it a different name. We call it the security survey. and here's what you're doing in the security survey number one target identification as part of target identification you're essentially saying what are our most valuable assets what data centers do we have what hardware do we have in those data centers what you know what different physical assets do we have that might be a target. This is where you're thinking about what are your valuable assets. In threat definition, you're thinking about what are the threats that are applicable to those assets. Could they're in a building? Could there be flooding, hurricanes, tornadoes, fires? theft, you're thinking about what are the threats that are applicable to the assets. And then the final thing that you're thinking about is facility characteristics. And this is fundamentally where you're thinking about what are the vulnerabilities associated. If you have a wood-framed building, it's gonna be vulnerable to fire. If you have a building that's built in a floodplain, it's gonna be vulnerable to flooding. So in the security survey, target identification, what are the valuable assets? Threat definition, what are the threats? Facility characteristics, what are the vulnerabilities? This is what's gonna help you figure out what are the risks and then therefore what would be the appropriate controls to mitigate those risks. as part of selecting the appropriate mitigating controls this is the site planning process that's what it's called the site planning process is where you're deciding which mitigating controls to put in place and there's a few major goals that you want to be thinking about as part of the site planning process the first most important thing that you always want to prioritize above all else in physical security is life, the safety of people. So when you're designing your physical security controls, you're going to make sure that those controls always prioritize the safety of people above all else. And then you're going to be thinking about how do we protect certain very valuable asset properties and certain key operations. And as part of this, one of the important things that you wanna do is think about where are your high value areas? Because obviously higher value areas are gonna, more controls are gonna be cost justified. So for instance, if you're a bank, your high value area is probably gonna be your bank vault. If you're a police station, it's probably gonna be your evidence locker. In office buildings, it's often the wiring closets. On each floor of an office building, there's usually a wiring closet with some switches and routers and stuff in there. If you're running a data center, it's gonna be your data center floor. So think about what are your high value areas? Where would more controls be cost justified? And let's start talking about a few of those controls, starting with the controls that you have around the perimeter of your building. And there's just a few that I wanna focus on here, starting with landscaping. Landscaping refers to the foliage around your building, the trees, the plants, and things like that. And the key thing that you wanna keep in mind with landscaping is you want You want the plants, the trees and stuff kept back away from your building. Because if you've got big trees, bushes, everything else like that growing right up to your building, you're not going to have good sight lines. Your camera systems, your guards are not going to be able to see if someone's sneaking up to your building. Or if you've got trees growing right beside your building, maybe they can climb up the tree and jump onto your roof. So... With landscaping, you want to keep the foliage back from the building, have good sight lines. And as an example of this, I've been to a few really secure facilities over the years and often super secure facilities are like out in the middle of a farm field in the middle of nowhere because you can see someone coming from miles. So that's the idea of landscaping. Grading is about how the ground is sloped. Now, would you want the ground sloped down towards your building or away from your building? Obviously, away from your building, right? If you get a huge amount of rain happening, if the ground is sloped down towards your building, you're gonna end up at the bottom of a lake. not good so you want the ground sloped away from your building and that way any water is naturally going to drain away from the building if there's huge flooding hopefully you're sitting nice and dry in the middle of a little island and not the bottom of a lake the last one i'll mention here physical security control is ballards what are ballards Ballards are big objects that are specifically designed to prevent vehicles from just driving through your building. The primary sort of preventative control that you're gonna have around your building is the walls of the building. And a really effective way of breaching the walls of a building is to just drive a big truck or car through the wall of the building or through the front doors of the building. And so if you want to prevent that, you can put bollards around your building. A great example of this, after the Oklahoma bombing, every US federal building in the US has to have bollards around it to prevent trucks, vehicles from getting anywhere near the building. You see this at US embassies. Go and look at Target stores. In front of every Target store, there's those giant red concrete balls in front. Those are there for a reason. They're bollards designed to prevent vehicle traffic from just driving through the front door of the building.
